title: The Defense Minister Russia Was Glad to See Go
subtitle: TASS and Kyiv Post arrived at the same factual conclusion about Ukraine's protests — for opposite reasons
body_md:
Russia's state news agency TASS published a sentence that few Western editors would have led with: "On Thursday, more than 1,000 people took part in protest rallies against Fyodorov's dismissal in at least 18 Ukrainian cities." [TASS](https://tass.com/world/2161431) had clear editorial incentives to amplify that number — Ukrainian domestic fracture is exactly the signal Moscow wants relayed globally. What makes the sentence remarkable is that [Kyiv Post](https://www.kyivpost.com/analysis/80433), Ukraine's centrist independent flagship, arrived at the same conclusion by a different route: "the political standoff has raised questions about whether Ukraine can avoid a deeper political crisis while fighting a full-scale war." Two outlets with opposite stakes in Ukraine's stability looked at the same demonstrations and agreed on the same facts.

When a state propaganda organ and a country's independent press of record converge on a single finding, that convergence is worth examining on its own terms. Across 73 articles from 30 outlets in 17 countries, the firing of defense minister Mykhailo Fedorov on July 15 produced a rare cross-factional consensus: the protests are real, geographically widespread, and politically consequential enough to constitute a crisis for Zelensky's wartime government.

The structural story most outlets agreed on was civil-military rupture. [AFP/France 24](https://www.france24.com/en/bitter-row-splits-ukraine-s-military-leadership-after-defence-minister-ousted) described "a bitter row" splitting Ukraine's military leadership, with Fedorov directing "stunning public criticism" at General Syrskyi, the army's top commander, on the day of his dismissal. [CBC World](https://www.cbc.ca/news/world/fedorov-ukraine-defence-9.7272575?cmp=rss) noted that Fedorov was Ukraine's fourth defence minister since the full-scale invasion — but "the first whose ouster has led to public backlash." Within 24 hours, demonstrators in Kyiv and Lviv had expanded their demands to include Syrskyi's resignation alongside Fedorov's reinstatement, transforming a personnel dispute into a broader referendum on the command structure.

German-language coverage foregrounded a frame nearly absent from English-language reporting: the conscription crisis as an underlying cause. [ZDF](https://www.zdfheute.de/politik/ausland/fedorow-entlassung-selenskyj-machtkampf-syrskyj-kabinett-umbau-ukraine-krieg-russland-100.html) examined what lay behind the power struggle — reporting on the deeper dynamics connecting Fedorov's removal to mounting tensions over mobilization law enforcement and the military's institutional resistance to Ukraine's expanded drone warfare doctrine. English-language editors treated the story as politics; German editors treated it as a structural military story.

Exiled Russian outlet [Meduza](https://meduza.io/en/feature/2026/07/17/ukrainians-protest-and-z-bloggers-celebrate-after-zelensky-fires-the-defense-minister-who-cut-russia-off-from-starlink-and-flooded-the-front-with-drones), publishing from Latvia, contributed the sharpest single headline in the corpus: "Ukrainians protest and Z-bloggers celebrate after Zelensky fires the defense minister who cut Russia off from Starlink and flooded the front with drones." Russian milbloggers' open celebration at Fedorov's removal — documented by Meduza — turned his departure into a battlefield intelligence signal. Fedorov was not simply a minister: he was the architect of Ukraine's asymmetric electronic warfare and drone supply advantage. That the Russian pro-war community and Russia's state media both publicly celebrated his dismissal is a data point that military analysts and wire reporters treated differently.

The document most revealing of Zelensky's position came not from a press release but from a presidential silence. [Ukrainska Pravda](https://www.pravda.com.ua/eng/news/2026/07/16/8044455/) was the only outlet to report that Zelensky's evening address named Yevhen Khmara as acting defense minister while making "no mention of protests in evening address" and citing only Khmara's long-range strike experience as justification. A president who addressed the nation and declined to acknowledge demonstrations in 18 cities made that omission deliberately. Ukrainska Pravda named it; the international press moved past it.

The paradox at the core of this corpus is structural rather than ideological. TASS needed the protests to be large because large protests signal Ukrainian instability to a global audience. Kyiv Post needed the protests to be legitimate because legitimate protests signal democratic accountability during wartime. Their conclusions pointed in opposite directions politically but converged on the same empirical claim: over a thousand people, eighteen cities, two consecutive days. The reader who encounters only one of these outlets sees the framing; the reader who encounters both sees the underlying fact they are jointly establishing — and cannot easily deny.

Khmara's appointment as acting defense minister was made without full parliamentary confirmation, a constitutional question [Kyiv Post](https://www.kyivpost.com/post/80435) flagged but no international outlet resolved. Ukraine is now managing an acting defense minister whose constitutional standing is disputed, a general staff accused by his predecessor of blocking drone procurement, and demonstrators in 18 cities who have received no official response. None of those tensions have been addressed. They have only been named.
